title: Why the South?
subtitle: Institutional Friction and the Spatial Reorganization of Data Center Infrastructure in the United States
title_normalized: why the south? institutional friction and the spatial reorganization of data center infrastructure in the united states

creators.name: Wu, Shaoyuan
creators.affiliation: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

publisher: Global AI Governance and Policy Research Center, EPINOVA LLC

alternate_identifiers.description: EPINOVA Working Paper D-Series publication number

description: This working paper explains why recent large-scale data center infrastructure expansion in the United States has increasingly clustered in the American South and selected interior regions. It advances a structural explanation centered on institutional feasibility, introducing a structural belt model and a three-axis framework of local institutional friction, utility buildability, and network interconnection to explain corridor-based hyperscale growth and its governance implications.
abstract: Despite common assumptions that large-scale data center infrastructure should concentrate in coastal technology hubs, low-tax jurisdictions, or energy-abundant regions, recent expansion in the United States follows a different spatial pattern. In the AI and hyperscale era, new large-scale development has increasingly clustered in the American South and selected interior regions. This paper addresses this puzzle by advancing a structural explanation centered on institutional feasibility rather than factor endowments alone. The paper introduces a structural belt model and a three-axis framework—local institutional friction, utility buildability, and network interconnection—to explain why certain regions function as low-resistance infrastructure zones. It shows that data center expansion follows a logic of friction minimization across governance, permitting, and power delivery systems, producing contiguous corridors of growth rather than isolated point-optimal sites. This spatial reorganization carries important governance implications. Rapid infrastructure lock-in in low-friction regions can outpace the adaptation of regulatory and political oversight, generating infrastructure–governance asymmetries. The findings contribute to debates on AI compute governance, infrastructure planning, and the political economy of large-scale digital systems.

files.description: Primary PDF file for EPINOVA Working Paper D–2026–02
files.label: Download PDF

series_information.series_scope: WP-D examines governance mechanisms and institutional design in AI-mediated systems, focusing on how infrastructure, authority, and decision processes generate lock-in, coordination burden, and failure risk and how such risks can be structurally contained.

methodology.approach: Conceptual and comparative structural analysis using a three-axis feasibility framework and structural belt model.
core_frameworks: Structural belt model of data center expansion

analytical_axes.name: Local Institutional Friction
analytical_axes.description: Captures zoning regimes, permitting procedures, litigation risk, and politicization of land-use decisions. Higher values indicate greater institutional resistance or uncertainty.

analytical_axes.name: Utility Buildability
analytical_axes.description: Measures the capacity of utilities and regulators to deliver large increments of power on predictable timelines, including grid capacity, interconnection procedures, transmission, and substation expansion.

analytical_axes.name: Network Interconnection
analytical_axes.description: Captures long-haul fiber access, carrier diversity, internet exchange access, redundancy, and scalable replication of network quality across sites.

formulas.name: Growth Attractiveness Index, static form
formulas.description: Measures baseline attractiveness for data center growth using utility buildability, network interconnection, and inverse local institutional friction.

formulas.name: Dynamic Growth Attractiveness Index
formulas.formula: D-GAI_t = [(α_t × B_t + β_t × C_t + γ_t × (5 − A_t)) / 5] × 100, with α_t + β_t + γ_t = 1
formulas.description: Generalizes the static GAI with phase-sensitive coefficients reflecting changing constraint dominance across development phases.

formulas.name: Infrastructure–Governance Asymmetry Pressure Index, static form
formulas.description: Measures the degree to which infrastructure expansion outpaces institutional mediation capacity.

formulas.name: Dynamic Infrastructure–Governance Asymmetry Pressure Index
formulas.formula: D-IGAP_t = 100 × [δ_t × (5 − A_t)^(−1) + ε_t × B_t + ζ_t × C_t], with δ_t + ε_t + ζ_t = 1
formulas.description: Represents how governance lag, scale acceleration, and network lock-in coefficients alter asymmetry pressure over time.

formulas.name: Infrastructure Friction Boundary
formulas.formula: α_t × B_i + β_t × C_i + γ_t × (5 − A_i) = Θ_t
formulas.description: Defines the dynamic feasibility threshold separating low-friction structural belts from structurally constrained zones.

methodology.case_logic: The paper uses stylized comparative state-level illustrations, including Northern Virginia, Georgia/Atlanta, Texas, and California, to validate the structural feasibility framework rather than to conduct large-N econometric estimation.

dimensions.name: Local Institutional Friction
dimensions.interpretation: Lower friction enables faster deployment, but prolonged low friction can generate infrastructure–governance asymmetry as lock-in outpaces oversight.

dimensions.name: Utility Buildability
dimensions.role: Power delivery and grid expansion capacity
dimensions.interpretation: Utility buildability matters more than nominal energy abundance because hyperscale deployment requires large loads to be delivered on predictable timelines.

dimensions.name: Network Interconnection
dimensions.interpretation: Robust and scalable interconnection supports corridor-based expansion when comparable network quality can be replicated across sites.

key_findings: Recent U.S. data center expansion is better explained by institutional feasibility than by coastal agglomeration, energy abundance, tax incentives, or single-factor optimization alone.
Large-scale data centers should be treated as lock-in infrastructure because they involve sunk capital, long time horizons, governance dependency, and difficult reversibility.
The American South and Southeast function as a low-friction structural belt because local institutional friction, utility buildability, and network interconnection align across contiguous areas.
Infrastructure expansion follows corridors of feasibility rather than isolated point-optimal sites because grids, fiber backbones, and logistics systems are spatially continuous.
Low-friction zones enable rapid deployment but also generate infrastructure–governance asymmetries when build-out outpaces regulatory, fiscal, environmental, and democratic oversight capacity.

Governance friction can serve as a temporal alignment mechanism rather than merely an obstruction, helping synchronize infrastructure expansion with public accountability and cumulative impact assessment.
AI compute governance increasingly depends on subnational institutional capacity because land use, power delivery, and infrastructure siting decisions occur where compute physically lands.

figures.title: Three-Axis Infrastructure Friction Boundary
figures.description: Conceptual illustration of the boundary separating low-friction structural belts from structurally constrained zones based on institutional friction (A), utility buildability (B), and network interconnection (C). Points indicate illustrative jurisdictional positions rather than empirical state-level observations. The boundary is dynamic and may shift as governance and infrastructure conditions evolve.
figures.source: Author’s conceptual visualization based on the three-axis feasibility framework.
figures.note: Conceptual illustration; not a regression-derived or empirical state-level frontier.

figures.title: Projecting the Infrastructure Friction Boundary onto the United States
figures.description: Stylized projection of the Infrastructure Friction Boundary onto U.S. geographic space. The apparent north–south orientation reflects the spatial manifestation of underlying institutional, utility, and network feasibility gradients rather than a fixed geographic, political, or historical dividing line.
figures.source: Author’s stylized projection based on the Infrastructure Friction Boundary model.

tables.title: Illustrative Phase-Specific Weighting Regimes
tables.description: Illustrative coefficient regimes for early expansion, rapid scaling, mature saturation, and post-backlash phases. The table shows how the relative weights of utility buildability, network interconnection, and governance friction shift as the dominant constraint changes.
